speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, August 8th, 1996 interview of Yeshoshua Trigor. Would you please tell us the name of the boat that you took from Italy to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, I did not know. But afterwards, a colleague in the foreign ministry who used to be in this business of transporting Jews to Palestine told me that the name of the boat was Nettuno, N-E-T-T-U-N-O. The boat was a boat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30 meters long. That's about 100 feet long, a small boat. Transported 200 Jews in terrible conditions-- it was very cramped, very full. And it-- I understand it made three trips. It was never discovered by the British. And it was taken out of the service by the Mossad [INAUDIBLE] because it was too small and costly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you most remember about this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: That some people, when they boarded the boat, thought that this was the small boat that will take them to the big boat, and that the big boat is somewhere in, in the ocean or in the, the Adriatic Sea or somewhere. Because it can, it cannot-- and the port is too small for this kind of ship. So that was a joke, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we asked people, OK, you want to go back? Said, no, no, no, I'll stay here. It was really difficult. And women were vomiting. And the, the sea was not very friendly at that time. And whenever they saw an airplane, a British airplane that was hunting for immigrant, circled in-- secret Aliyah, illegal Aliyah, ships-- then everybody had to go down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, they used to raise a flag-- I don't know, a Greek flag, or a Turkish flag, or whatever flag. And they acted this fisherman. And nobody was on the deck. So when everybody was down, it was very cramped and it was very difficult to breathe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month of 1945 did you take this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how long did this trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, about 10 to 12 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you reached Palestine, what was-- do you remember your initial reaction to landing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- everything was as if I was there before. Because I, I read, and I saw photographs, and I read books from childhood. I knew exactly where I was. And we went out of the, of the vessel. And, and we walked. They brought us up to walking possibility. And we walked to the Holy Land in wet and very happy. And the welcoming committee was there. And they-- we slept one night. And they I, I had a sister. And I gave them the address. So they gave me two Israeli-- two, two British pounds. And they sent me on my way. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in contact with any of the people that you had traveled from Vilna to Palestine with after you reached Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, some-- maybe some of the time we were together. I kept mainly in contact with my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And what was your sister's reaction when she saw you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, she was very, very happy. And I stayed with them a very short time. And then I saw that their apartment, their little house, was not suitable for, for another grownup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Petah Tikva.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I found a, a room in, in Tel Aviv, and also before that, near Ramat Gan. I found a job in the Ramat Gan local counsel, afterwards with the state controller, and then the top in the foreign ministry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you reach the foreign ministry, did you continue your education in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, at the Tel Aviv School of Law and Economics, which is today part of the Tel Aviv University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what years did you attend that school?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the foreign ministry, I attended National, National Political and Defense College in 1956, '57.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What do you recall of the War of Independence for Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was at that time still sick. And I was still hurting. I was-- my foot and some-- one lung and my nose were. So well, it, it was a terrible time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these illnesses cause you to spend time in a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I was under constant doctor's attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, how did you get involved in the foreign ministry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was working for the state controller. I went to South Africa for personal reasons, to, to get back some money that was owed to my parents. And while I was there, I learned English. It was six, seven month. When I came back, I applied to the foreign ministry. I got a very lowly job in the-- the lowest that you can-- in the Embassy of Israel in Sydney. They sent me to Sydney. And while there, I learned the job. I had a very good boss, very good ambassador, Max Nurock, an Irish Jewish. And he was very helpful to me. And when I came back in '56, they sent me four years to National Defense and Political School in Jerusalem. When I graduated from that school, we were 24 students, of which five were from the foreign ministry and others were from other government departments.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of the countries that you served in in the foreign ministry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I served in, in Australia and New Zealand, in Atlanta, Georgia, in Los Angeles, then in Seoul, Republic of Korea, in the Netherlands, in India-- before we had an embassy, we had a consulate in Bombay, not in Delhi-- in, in Malta, and then again in Atlanta, Georgia as counsel general and in Miami, Florida as counsel general. And I had five independent posts-- Korea, Netherlands, Malta, southeast Atlanta, and Miami. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which of these nations that you served in did you find the most challenging for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one of the most difficult jobs was to keep the Israeli flag in India, which-- Indian subcontinent. We were the only ones there. There, there was nothing in Pakistan. There was, after a while, nothing in Ceylon, Sri Lanka. And India was under great Arab pressure to close the consulate, especially in the '73 war. But India did not close the consulate. And I was very proud to, to have survived the four years in India, which was very difficult politically and also physically.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what years were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from July, 1973, till October, 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the physical problems that you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: The physical problems were problems of health. And you know, it's very difficult there with water and so on. And the other problems are very well-known, very well-documented.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you retire from the foreign service?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your main interests and activities since retiring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I lecture a great deal. I write to Florida and Georgia newspapers, and also to Fort Lauderdale newspaper, to to and also to some Jewish publications in Switzerland. And it's political commentary, where I always analyze political events and present them from the Jewish point of view, from the Israeli point of view. I also was interested and am interested in the-- what kind of a message to leave to posterity about the Holocaust. And I were not-- was not very happy with what was presented to the world. And this is why I wrote my memoirs and on which a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] screenplay, is based. I hope that what I wrote-- and you have seen one of my articles-- brings a little more balance to Jewish-Gentile relationships before and after the war. What I'm trying to say is this. Most of the presentations I saw or read start with the war, bombings, bombings, entry of the German army, Jews sent to ghettos, segregated, killed, molested, sent to camps, gassed. And sometimes a decent non-Jew is helping them. My own story, which I underline also in my memoirs, about how I helped to Gentiles before one of them helped me, I hope brings a little more balance into Jewish-German relationships. Because I believe-- and Jewish-Gentle relationships-- because I believe there are many Jews that I heard from my lady friend that in a case-- of a case that her, her neighbor met a Polish farmer before the war and saw him crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it so happens that his horse dropped dead. So he wanted to kill himself. He said, what am I going to do without a horse? How can I feed my family? How can I run my farm? So this fellow gave him 100 zloty. And he bought a new horse. When the war started, this person came to him and took him to the farm. And saved him in the family. Now, not all the Gentiles returned the favor. But some did.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at least I want to show also the Jewish deeds. Because I believe that the Jews and any Jew who survived the war without having helped any others Jew is a hero. Not every Jew could fight with weapons in his hence hands. He didn't have the opportunity. But every Jew who survived is a hero. And every Jew's story is unique and should be kept for posterity.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you mentioned your lady friend. Would you please say her name and spell her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Judy, Judith, J-U-D-I-T-H, W-I-L-F, Wilf. You want me to bring her in?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll do that in a few minutes. Do you keep in touch with anyone from your hometown at the present time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I keep in touch with some people from my hometown that are in Israel and also from other parts of Lithuania that live in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you ever have dreams about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first years after I arrived in Israel, I certainly had. But with time passing, this kind of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] disappeared. And I couldn't say that I dream about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel your experiences in the Holocaust have influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe that the, the big lesson is that Israel was established three years-- 10 years too late. If Israel was established earlier and if the Jewish people went to Israel and built Israel, if rabbis would have sent their flock to Israel, and of course, if we were more-- and this, I cannot say whether it was possible-- to fight better the British blockade had more people and earlier have a state. I don't know whether it was possible. But of course, this would have saved us probably a few million Jews. Because if there was an Israel-- there was no place for Jews to go. The ship St. Louis went around the world and came back to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the [INAUDIBLE] Conference-- I believe the expression, name-- they dealt and dealt and talked and talked. And no country was prepared to name and figure out how much-- how many Jews they can take, including the United States. So because of that, my lesson from the Holocaust is that only a strong Israel can survive and be a guarantee for those people who may need it one day. What do you feel the world should learn from the Holocaust period?

speaker: interviewee
text: The world should learn that because there was a Holocaust and it was not checked and was not prevented, the result to Auschwitz was not bombed, the priorities are such that the Holocaust survival was not a main interest. Or as the British liberal government of Baron [INAUDIBLE] sad, if Jews will survive, what we going to do with them? They will want to go to Palestine. We don't want them to go to Palestine, because the Arabs object to it. So you asked me--

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked, what do you feel the world should learn--

speaker: interviewee
text: The world should know that because they did not stop this Holocaust, maybe other cases of murdering people occurred. Of course, I don't compare what happened in Bosnia to what compare-- what happened to us. But I say that the license to kill was given.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. It's been an honor to interview you today. Yehoshua, will you introduce your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my friend, my dear friend, Judy Wilf.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Judy, when and how did you learn about Yehoshua's experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since we met-- I guess it's, like, three and a half years ago. I-- we were talking about it all the time. Because in all the Holocaust survivors, wherever they are-- at a party or any gathering-- no matter how festive it is, at the end, they start talking about the Holocaust. This goes on for years already. When we met, we started to talk about it right away, of course. Because in a way, I'm also a Holocaust survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you a Holocaust survivor also?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I spent years of the war in, in Russia, in Siberia. Wasn't-- I cannot compare it to the ghettos and concentration camps, because I was free. But I was away from my family, far away. And also, the officials were coming all the time. And they were threatening us that we never-- we will never be able to leave Siberia. This is the place where we will end our lives. It was very depressing. And-- but if you-- like, hypnotized by, by it if they come to you three times a week. So you think it's true. At this age, I was more impressionable, I guess. I-- and I kind of-- you get so depressed. But when the war was over, we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How do you feel the Holocaust has affected your life and your relationship with Yehoshua?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust affected me personally. I lost my whole family, my grandma and parents and three brothers and two sisters. My two brothers were married during the war. One had two children. And one had one baby. And they all got killed, not on the list. I don't know where. I don't know when. I don't know if they were, were buried or not. So it's terrible to think about and live with it. First of all, I see them all always alive. I never saw them dead or sick. So to me, they're always alive. And I reminisce and talk about them like they are alive. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What lessons do you feel the world should learn from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: We should learn a big lesson that we should strive for peace. And we should do everything in our power to teach the people not to have so much hatred in, in them. Why are there so mad at the Jews, mad at other-- then they are mad at each other, too, once they start with us. It's a terrible lesson to, to learn. But also, for, for posterity, it's very important to know about the Holocaust. Because right after the war, nobody wanted to know about it. Took maybe 20-some years, 25 years for the people to even-- that wanted to know about it or, or talk about it. If my friends that came back from, from the camps wanted to learn the story to their, to their relatives in America or other countries, they said, don't tell me these horror stories. I cannot take it. Nobody wanted to listen to it. Nobody wanted to know about it. But now, it's surfaced. And everybody's talking about it. And so many books came out. And so it's a different story, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank both of you for taking part in this project. It's been and honor to know both of you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is the photo of my late father, Jakov Leib Trigobovas, and my mother, Esther Trigobovas, nee Feinberg, done in Lithuania in the 1920s or beginning of the 1930s. I was in my hometown in 1993. I actually wanted to see my mother's and other Jews' mass graves. And at the time, I identified myself to the Lithuanian driver as a Jew born there. And he said, that's impossible. Because there were no Jews in Mazeikiai. I said, well, you think again. Because it was 47 years ago. And he went and spoke to his father. And his father said, well, here's a postcard. And here is the street where your client, your have customer used to live. And I can see the house where we used to live. It's right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that on the left or the right hand side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, on the left side, the two-story mural-- I mean, the brick house on the left side at the end. It is taken from the railroad station square. You can see the fountain. And you can see the, the water fountain for, for drinking. This is a photo of Mazeikiai state high school taken in 1934 on the occasion of the visit of the minister of education, [INAUDIBLE]. And it was given to me by girls, Jewish girls, who spent the war in Russia and who took the photographs with them. And they gave it to me while I was in Israel. I recognize here all my teachers and the principal and the minister. There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is of the wedding of my nephew, [? Jakov ?] Firestein, fourth from right. And next to him is my late sister, Gita, passed away in 1989, my brother-in-law, [INAUDIBLE] Firestein, and in the corner, myself, participating in the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Prime Minister Levi Eshkol, first from left, was visiting the United States at the invitation of President Johnson in 1964. And he is pictured here on the left. Next to him, Counsel General [INAUDIBLE], the Costa Rican ambassador, and myself, Yehoshua Trigor. In the foreign ministry, , one of my tasks as a German speaker was to escort important guests from German-speaking countries, certainly from Germany. This is a photograph where you can see on my right, Adenauer's older daughter, and on my left, Dr. Konrad Adenauer. The position of many people in Israel was that it was too early to have German visitors. Because Adenauer personally was regarded as a person who tried to make peace between the Jews and Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1966 in Jerusalem. In 1969, I was appointed [INAUDIBLE] of the Israel in Seoul, Korea. And this photo is taken at a reception for my departing colleague, [INAUDIBLE], second from left. And on the extreme left is the Brazilian ambassador. On the right is the representative of the United Nations' Development program in in, in, in Korea. This is one of the last photos of Golda Meir of blessed memory. She was no more prime minister. She was sick. And a representative of Basic Black American [INAUDIBLE] Community, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], they insisted that I bring Golda Meir. And she was kind enough to come and to address the group at the Hilton Hotel in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in '78 or '79, prior to her demise. Prime Minister Shamir is greeting a delegation of American Jews-- actually, the Los Angeles Jewish Federation. And I am second from right, introducing him to the director of the federation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in the '80s, early '80s. Maybe you can cut down on this. This is a photocopy of a letter sent to me by President Carter after we discussed for a couple of hours the establishment of the Carter Center in Atlanta, connected with the Emory University and what the countries that had some connection with his presidency, and in this case Israel and Egypt. I mean, President Sadat and Prime Minister Begin made the agreement under the auspices of President Carter. And a few years later, he came to discuss it. And he sent me a letter of thanks about-- as he said, for the advice and concern I expressed in the meeting. The date, you can see on the [INAUDIBLE]. This is one of the occasions when I met with the American VIPs during public meetings where we addressed the Jewish concerns. And here's a photograph, on the left with Al Haig, General Haig, Commander of NATO, secretary of state, a great American, and a great friend of Israel. His wife, a wonderful woman in her own right, is in the center. During UJA meetings, we met very interesting people. Here is the member of the defense committee of the Congress, Senator Ted Stevens, a great friend of Israel, speaking to Judy Wilf and myself. It was taken in 1995, I believe in Palm Beach.
